whoToFollow.reason: Leading the read layer overhaul for Solana.
Core steward of Solana’s ecosystem and infrastructure.

content.signal: Solana is partnering with Triton One to overhaul its foundational read layer as the network hits new transaction and volume highs.
content.story:
Solana is entering a critical new phase of infrastructure development. Triton One has announced a partnership with the Solana Foundation to completely rebuild Solana’s read layer, a core component that has remained largely untouched since the network’s launch. This move comes as Solana’s transaction counts, DEX volume, and stablecoin activity reach unprecedented levels, putting new pressure on the network’s underlying architecture.

### What's Happening

While Solana’s execution and networking layers have seen significant upgrades—thanks to initiatives like Firedancer and Anza—the read layer has lagged behind. Originally built as an extension of the validator, it was never designed to handle the scale Solana now faces. With over 25 billion transactions in Q1 2026, and DEX volumes surpassing $284 billion in a single quarter, the old architecture is showing its age.

The partnership with Triton One aims to address this by redesigning how data is accessed and served to users and applications. This is not just a technical refresh; it’s a foundational shift that will impact everything from wallet performance to analytics and dApp reliability. The overhaul is expected to roll out by 2026, aligning with Solana’s rapid ecosystem expansion.

### Why It Matters

Solana’s growth is not just a story of more users or higher prices—it’s about real, on-chain activity. The network now processes more transactions than all other top chains combined, and its DEX market share has climbed to 41%, outpacing Ethereum and all L2s together. Stablecoin supply on Solana has ballooned from under $2 billion in 2024 to over $14 billion in 2026, with major banks like Lead Bank and Cross River now settling USDC directly on-chain.

This surge in usage is driving demand for more robust infrastructure. As tokenized assets, lending protocols, and new financial primitives proliferate, the need for fast, reliable data access becomes critical. The read layer upgrade is a strategic move to ensure Solana can support not just today’s scale, but the next wave of adoption—including new features like encrypted dApps and integration with mainstream platforms like X (formerly Twitter).

### The Risk or Debate

Overhauling a core part of the network is not without risk. Any major architectural change introduces the potential for new bugs, outages, or unforeseen performance issues. The transition period could be especially sensitive, as Solana is now a key settlement layer for banks, stablecoins, and tokenized assets. If the new read layer fails to deliver, it could undermine confidence in the network’s reliability.

There’s also debate about whether Solana’s rapid scaling can continue without hitting new bottlenecks elsewhere. While the read layer is being addressed, other components may need similar attention as usage patterns evolve. The ecosystem’s reliance on a small number of core teams for critical upgrades is another point of discussion.

### What To Watch

The rollout of the new read layer will be a major milestone for Solana. Watch for technical updates from Triton One and the Solana Foundation on migration plans, testing phases, and performance benchmarks. Adoption by major dApps and wallets will be a key signal of success.

Keep an eye on how the upgrade impacts user experience, especially during periods of high network activity. Also, monitor whether other chains respond with similar infrastructure investments as Solana sets a new standard for throughput and usability.
takeaways: Solana is partnering with Triton One to rebuild its core read layer for better scalability.
Network activity and on-chain volume are at all-time highs, stressing current infrastructure.
The upgrade is critical but carries risks around stability and transition management.

whoToFollow.reason: Provides timely updates and analysis on Solana ecosystem campaigns.
Official source for campaign details and platform updates.

content.signal: JupiterExchange users can now claim over $200K in mystery rewards, with additional $50K in TGC incentives live for active traders.
content.story:
JupiterExchange has launched a new phase of user rewards, distributing over $200,000 in mystery airdrops to eligible users. The claim window is limited to 31 days, requiring users to check eligibility through the Jupiter Mobile app or browser extension. In parallel, Jupiter has rolled out another Trading Game Campaign (TGC), offering $50,000 in rewards over a two-week period for those trading PreStocks in Ultra Mode.

### What's Happening

Eligible JupiterExchange users can access the Rewards Hub to determine if they qualify for a share of the $200K+ mystery airdrop. The process involves importing a wallet into Jupiter's mobile or extension platform and checking for available rewards. Separately, the new TGC incentivizes trading activity: for every $2,000 in PreStocks volume in Ultra Mode, users receive a reward card, with card values ranging from $1 up to $10,000. Both initiatives are time-bound, with the airdrop claim window set at 31 days and the TGC running for two weeks.

### Why It Matters

Jupiter's ongoing reward campaigns signal an aggressive push to deepen user engagement and liquidity on Solana. The scale of the airdrop and the gamified trading incentives reflect a broader trend of exchanges using direct rewards to retain active users and attract new participants. These campaigns may also help Jupiter differentiate itself in a crowded Solana ecosystem, where user loyalty and sustained activity are critical for protocol growth.

The mystery element of the airdrop and the randomized card values in the TGC introduce an element of unpredictability, which can boost participation and social sharing. By requiring wallet imports and active trading, Jupiter is also likely gathering valuable user data and reinforcing its mobile and extension platforms as primary access points.

### The Risk or Debate

Short claim windows and gamified reward structures can create pressure for users to act quickly, potentially leading to rushed decisions or missed opportunities. The randomized nature of rewards may also generate disappointment among users who receive lower-value cards or miss out on eligibility. Additionally, frequent reward campaigns could dilute the perceived value of future incentives or raise questions about long-term sustainability.

### What To Watch

The immediate focus is on how many users claim the airdrop and participate in the TGC. Uptake rates and social sentiment will offer early signals about campaign effectiveness. It is also worth monitoring whether Jupiter introduces further reward rounds, adjusts eligibility criteria, or expands the range of incentivized trading products. User feedback on the claim process and reward distribution may influence future campaign design.
takeaways: JupiterExchange is distributing over $200K in mystery airdrop rewards to eligible users.
A new two-week Trading Game Campaign offers $50K in rewards for PreStocks trading.
Both campaigns are time-limited, emphasizing quick participation and engagement.

whoToFollow.reason: Flagged the multisig issue and drives discussion on Solana security.
Provides on-chain data and analysis relevant to Raydium’s setup.

content.signal: Raydium, a major Solana DEX, manages large TVL with a 2/3 multisig, prompting renewed scrutiny over protocol security.
content.story:
A fresh wave of attention is hitting Raydium, one of Solana’s leading decentralized exchanges, after users highlighted its use of a 2/3 multisig to control protocol assets. With significant total value locked (TVL) on the platform, questions are surfacing about whether this security setup is robust enough for such a critical DeFi infrastructure.

### What's Happening

Raydium’s core contracts and treasury are reportedly governed by a 2/3 multisig wallet. This means that any two out of three designated signers can approve and execute transactions affecting protocol funds or upgrades. The issue was flagged publicly, sparking debate about whether this level of multisig security is appropriate for a platform of Raydium’s scale.

The discussion is not new in DeFi, but it’s gaining traction as Raydium’s TVL grows and as more users rely on its liquidity. References to on-chain data and dashboards, such as those shared by community members, are fueling the conversation and drawing in analysts and builders from across the Solana ecosystem.

### Why It Matters

Multisig wallets are a standard security measure in DeFi, but the number of signers and the threshold for approvals are critical details. A 2/3 setup can be efficient for operations, but it also means that a compromise of just two signers could put all protocol funds at risk. For a platform with high TVL, this risk profile stands out.

The debate reflects a broader tension in DeFi between operational agility and security. As protocols mature and attract more capital, the community expects stronger, more decentralized controls. How Raydium and similar projects address these concerns will influence user trust and the broader perception of Solana DeFi’s resilience.

### The Risk or Debate

The core risk is that a 2/3 multisig may not provide enough protection against internal collusion or targeted attacks. With only three signers, the compromise or malicious action of two individuals could have catastrophic consequences. This setup also raises questions about transparency—users may not know who the signers are or how they are selected.

Some argue that higher thresholds or more distributed multisig setups (such as 4/7 or larger councils) are warranted for protocols with substantial TVL. Others point out that increasing the number of signers can slow down governance and response times, which can be a trade-off in fast-moving markets.

### What To Watch

Community pressure could prompt Raydium to review and potentially upgrade its multisig structure. Look for official statements or proposals from the Raydium team addressing these concerns. The broader Solana DeFi ecosystem may also see renewed discussions about best practices for protocol security and governance.

Any moves toward more decentralized or transparent multisig setups could set new standards for other projects. Users and analysts will be watching closely for changes in Raydium’s security model, as well as for any incidents that might validate or challenge the current debate.
takeaways: Raydium controls protocol assets with a 2/3 multisig wallet.
Concerns are rising about whether this setup is secure enough for high TVL.
The debate highlights ongoing trade-offs between security and operational efficiency in DeFi.
